subject: Economics
contextNote: JC1 Economics, 24 students. You modelled PED = %ΔQd ÷ %ΔP using a worked example — a 10% rise in price leads to a 4% fall in quantity demanded, giving PED = −0.4 — and showed how this connects to the steepness of the demand curve and to tax policy: inelastic demand means consumers bear most of the tax burden. Students have been given a task: 'A 10% rise in price leads to a 4% fall in quantity demanded. Calculate PED. Draw the demand curve. Explain what this means for a government considering a tax on this good.' You have circulated for 4 minutes and noted what each student attempted first and what they left blank.

students.name: Rajan
students.note: Applied the formula immediately and correctly: PED = −0.4. Has written 'inelastic, consumers bear tax burden.' Demand curve left entirely blank — no graph attempted.

students.name: Nur Atiqah
students.note: Drew a steep demand curve accurately and labelled it 'inelastic.' Has not attempted the calculation at all. Written 'steep means inelastic' beside the graph.

students.name: Wei Jie
students.note: Has written 'inelastic means steep, government should tax this good' — verbally correct and well-reasoned. No numbers, no diagram attempted.

students.name: Siti
students.note: Partial graph (axes labelled, curve drawn at wrong gradient — too flat for inelastic). Formula written but numerator and denominator reversed: %ΔP ÷ %ΔQd. Explanation blank.

ep1.situation: 25 minutes into the lesson. You circulate for 4 minutes and the pattern is striking — not a readiness gap between students, but a representation gap within each student. Rajan has applied the formula immediately and precisely: PED = −4% ÷ 10% = −0.4. He has written 'inelastic — consumers bear the tax burden' as his policy implication. His work is correct and complete in the numerical and verbal dimensions. But his demand curve space is entirely blank. He has not attempted the graph at all. Nur has drawn a steep demand curve with carefully labelled axes and the correct gradient for inelastic demand. She has written 'steep means inelastic' beside it. But she has not written a single number. The calculation space is blank — she has not attempted the formula, and her policy sentence is missing. Wei Jie has written a clear, well-reasoned policy argument: 'Inelastic demand means consumers are not very sensitive to price changes — so a government tax on this good would not reduce quantity demanded much, making it an effective source of revenue.' His verbal reasoning is accurate and well-structured. But there are no numbers and no diagram anywhere on his page. Siti has attempted all three representations, but each is incomplete or incorrect. Her demand curve has the axes labelled correctly, but the gradient is too flat — she has drawn what looks like an elastic demand curve for an inelastic good. Her formula is written down, but the numerator and denominator are reversed: she has written %ΔP ÷ %ΔQd instead of %ΔQd ÷ %ΔP. Her explanation space is blank. A quick scan of the class: 9 students have attempted all three representations. 8 have attempted only one. 7 have attempted two. The goal is not to confirm each student's preferred mode — it is to get all three representations connected and internally consistent. 20 minutes remain.
ep1.evidence: The gap is not in ability or conceptual understanding — each student can demonstrate genuine understanding in at least one representation. Rajan has the formula and the policy reasoning but not the graph. Nur has the graph but not the formula or policy sentence. Wei Jie has the verbal reasoning but no numbers and no diagram. Siti has attempted all three but has errors in two — her formula is reversed and her graph gradient is wrong, suggesting a conceptual gap in connecting the representations rather than just arithmetic. The challenge is to use each student's strength as a bridge toward the representations they are avoiding, not to validate single-representation thinking.

choices.label: Two-phase representation check: prompt all three, then fill the gap
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and give a two-phase prompt without stopping student work for long. Phase 1: 'Check you have all three — a number, a diagram, and a sentence. You don't need all three to be perfect yet. Just check what you have.' Give 30 seconds for students to look at their own work. Phase 2: 'For whichever one you left blank — start it now. Use what you already have as your entry point. If you have the formula, use the number to draw the curve. If you have the graph, use the slope to figure out the formula. If you have the sentence, use the idea to try the calculation.' Give 3 minutes and circulate. Focus specifically on Rajan, who has no graph, and Siti, whose formula is reversed. For Rajan, prompt: 'You have PED = −0.4 — what does that tell you about the steepness of the curve?' For Siti, prompt: 'Which is the quantity change and which is the price change in your formula?'
choices.tradeoff: The two-phase check is low-threat — it uses each student's existing work as a scaffold into the representation they are missing, rather than announcing a new task. Students who have one representation correct can use it as the entry point for the second. But the prompt is permissive enough to allow avoidance: Wei Jie may write another verbal sentence rather than attempting the calculation or drawing a diagram. The prompt works best with targeted follow-up for students who interpret 'start it' as 'write more of the same.' Without individual follow-up, Siti's formula reversal may go unaddressed if she continues writing the reversed formula with more confidence.

choices.label: Structured representation triads: each student explains their strength
choices.elaboration: Form triads of three students — one anchored in each representation where possible. Assign: one student with a strong formula (like Rajan), one with a strong graph (like Nur), one with a strong verbal explanation (like Wei Jie). Give each student in the triad 2 minutes to explain their representation to the other two: 'Explain what you did and why it tells you something about PED.' The other two must listen specifically to fill the gap in their own work — not to evaluate their partner's explanation but to build their own missing representation. Siti sits in a triad with two students whose representations complement her partial attempts. Brief the protocol before triads begin: 'You are listening to fill your own gap — when your partner finishes, your job is to add the representation you are missing to your own answer.'
choices.tradeoff: Representation triads leverage each student's strength purposefully — Rajan's formula explanation may help Siti correct her numerator/denominator reversal more accessibly than a direct teacher correction, because it comes through a peer working through the same problem. Nur's graph explanation gives Rajan an entry point into the visual representation from someone who drew it before calculating. But students may explain their preferred mode without attending to what the others need to receive — the protocol must be briefed clearly: 'You are listening to fill your own gap, not to assess your partner.' Siti's formula reversal is a conceptual error rather than an arithmetic one, and a peer explanation may not correct it if the peer assumes the reversal is an arithmetic slip rather than a misunderstanding of what the formula measures.

choices.label: IRF (Initiate-Response-Feedback) representation chain: connect all three representations publicly
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and run a whole-class IRF chain structured around the three representations. Start with Nur: 'Tell me what your diagram shows about this good's demand.' (Initiation — graphical). After Nur describes the steep curve, ask a different student: 'Can you express what Nur just showed us using the formula — what number do you get?' (Response — numerical). Then ask Wei Jie: 'Connect both: what does this number and this curve tell a government that is considering a tax on this good?' (Feedback — verbal). The rule for the chain: do not confirm any student's answer until they have expressed it in at least two representations. After the chain, ask all students: 'Check your own work — does your number match your curve? Does your curve match your policy sentence? Find the link you cannot make yet.' Cold-call by position, not volunteer — start with Nur to establish the visual, then draw out the formula from a student who did not go first, then Wei Jie for the policy implication. Brief a recovery prompt for Siti: 'Does this match your diagram? What is the same?'
choices.tradeoff: The IRF chain forces cross-representation connection publicly and builds a shared classroom language for the relationship between the three forms of PED. It makes visible what it means to have all three representations consistent — not just each one being correct in isolation. Cold-calling by position ensures students who avoided certain representations are prompted to engage with them. But the chain puts students on the spot in front of the class, which risks embarrassing students who are not yet ready to work in a different mode. Siti may not be ready to contribute in any representation at this stage — her graph gradient is wrong and her formula is reversed, so she cannot anchor the chain in either. Rajan's graph gap will only be addressed if he is directly asked to draw the curve described by others rather than allowed to contribute only in the formula mode.

A.consequence: The two-phase prompts produce uneven results. After Phase 1, 18 of 24 students have attempted all three representations — up from 9 at the baseline. But the quality of the new attempts varies significantly. Rajan has drawn a demand curve — the gradient is roughly correct for an inelastic good, steep rather than flat, but imprecise. He has not labelled the axes or annotated the curve with any explanation of why the gradient communicates inelastic demand. Nur has attempted the formula for the first time. She has written %ΔQd ÷ %ΔP = 4% ÷ 10% — the correct method — but has written the answer as 0.4, dropping the negative sign. Her calculation structure is right; she has missed the sign convention. Wei Jie has attempted a calculation for the first time, writing %ΔQd ÷ %ΔP = 4% ÷ 10% with the correct method, but his arithmetic gives 0.25 — a slip, not a conceptual error. He has not drawn a diagram. Siti's formula is still reversed after the Phase 2 prompt — she continues to write %ΔP ÷ %ΔQd. When you asked her 'Which is the quantity change and which is the price change?', she looked at the formula and said 'The quantity change is the 4%' — correctly identifying the quantity change — but then placed it on the bottom of the fraction again. Her graph gradient is now corrected after a peer beside her pointed to the board model. Representation attempts are up. Cross-representation accuracy is still weak. 18 minutes remain.

choices.label: Board representation chain — model, erase, reconnect
choices.elaboration: Stop the class for 90 seconds. Write the complete three-representation chain on the board in three clearly labelled columns: 'Number: PED = %ΔQd ÷ %ΔP = −4% ÷ 10% = −0.4.' 'Diagram: steep demand curve (label the gradient, label the axes, mark the price rise and quantity fall).' 'Policy reasoning: PED < 1 → inelastic → consumers bear most of the tax burden → government taxes this good to maximise revenue.' Emphasise two specific points: 'The negative sign matters — it tells you quantity fell when price rose. A steep curve means a small quantity response — that is what PED < 1 looks like.' Then erase the board immediately and ask students to reconnect their own three representations: 'Does your number match your curve? Does your curve match your sentence? Find the link you cannot make — that is what you are working on for the next 15 minutes.' For Rajan specifically, prompt in-flight: 'Your diagram is now roughly correct. If the government doubles the tax, what happens to tax revenue — and can you show that on your diagram as an area?'
choices.tradeoff: The board model makes the three-way chain explicit, concrete, and complete — it shows exactly what internal consistency across three representations looks like. Erasing it immediately means students must reconstruct the connection in their own work rather than copy from the board, which forces active integration rather than passive transcription. The model addresses Nur's sign error directly ('the negative sign matters') and gives Wei Jie the formula structure to correct his arithmetic. But Rajan, who already has the formula and policy reasoning, is given an in-flight extension that requires no new resource — it uses his existing correct formula as the starting point for a more demanding application. Siti's formula reversal is a conceptual error that the board model may not correct — she needs to understand why quantity is the numerator, not just see the correct formula displayed.

choices.label: AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) cross-representation peer critique: does the number match the curve match the sentence?
choices.elaboration: Ask pairs to swap their full written answers — all three representations together. Give the AQS structure specifically for cross-representation consistency: 'Affirm — which of the three representations is your partner's strongest? Tell them what is correct. Question — does the number match the diagram? Does the diagram match the policy sentence? Find where they are inconsistent. Suggest — one specific correction that would make all three consistent.' The key difference from standard AQS is the focus on consistency between representations, not just correctness within each. Circulate specifically toward Siti's pair — her formula reversal may be caught by a partner with a correct calculation. If Siti's partner affirms the reversed formula, intervene: 'Which number is the price change and which is the quantity change? Which one goes on top in the formula?' Give 4 minutes, then ask one pair to share the consistency question they found — not the answer, but the gap they identified.
choices.tradeoff: AQS cross-representation critique forces students to check consistency across all three representations rather than evaluating each one in isolation — a student can have a correct verbal explanation of inelastic demand and a correct graph, but if those are paired with a formula that gives PED > 1, the inconsistency is caught by the Question step. This targets the specific challenge of the lesson: not each representation being right individually, but all three being connected and coherent. But pairs where both students have the same error — for example, both missing the negative sign — may not catch it through peer review alone. Teacher circulation is essential, particularly for Siti, whose formula reversal may be reinforced if her partner validates rather than questions it.

B.consequence: The triads work through their explanations. Rajan explains his formula to Nur and Wei Jie with precision: 'PED is the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price. The 4% fall in quantity goes on top, the 10% price rise goes on the bottom — so PED = −4% ÷ 10% = −0.4. The negative sign tells you quantity fell when price rose.' Nur listens and writes the formula correctly in her notes, including the negative sign. When she attempts it herself, she gets −0.4. Her formula is now correct. Nur then explains her graph to the other two: 'A steep demand curve means that for a big move in price, quantity barely changes — which is what inelastic means. The slope of the curve shows the ratio.' Rajan draws a demand curve on his page for the first time and labels the axes correctly. The gradient is now precise, not approximate. Wei Jie hears Rajan's formula explanation and corrects his arithmetic slip — he gets −0.4. He still writes his policy sentence first before attempting any other representation, but the calculation is now correct. Siti hears the formula explained twice — once by Rajan, once by a student in her triad who explains it a different way. But when she works alone to redo her formula after the triad, she still writes %ΔP ÷ %ΔQd — the reversal persists. When asked why, she says: 'I thought price was the thing we are measuring the change of.' The formula reversal is a conceptual error about what PED measures, not an arithmetic error or a memory failure. 19 minutes remain.

choices.label: IRF (Initiate-Response-Feedback) cross-representation probe: use graph fluency as the bridge to formula
choices.elaboration: Nominate Nur directly: 'Nur, you drew the correct curve before you calculated. Tell me — how does the steepness of the curve tell you something about the size of the PED number? What is the connection?' Build the IRF chain using Nur's graphical strength as the explanatory entry point for the formula — reversing the typical order of instruction. From Nur's response, extend to Wei Jie: 'Wei Jie, if I told you PED = −2.5, describe the demand curve without drawing it. What would it look like?' Then extend to Rajan: 'Rajan — connect both: if the curve is flat, what does that tell a government about taxing this good and why?' After the IRF chain, direct one question at Siti: 'Siti, complete this sentence independently in writing: A steeper demand curve means PED is ___ because ___.' Give her 90 seconds to write. Her sentence will reveal whether the conceptual link between curve steepness and the size of PED has formed — or whether she is still separating the representations in her thinking.
choices.tradeoff: Using Nur's graphical strength as the explanatory entry point for the formula reverses the typical order — instead of 'here is the formula, now draw the curve,' the chain asks 'here is the curve, what does it tell you about the formula?' This may help Siti understand why quantity is the numerator: because PED measures the quantity response to a price change, not the price response to a quantity change. Wei Jie's verbal fluency is tested against a specific number, which pushes him from qualitative to quantitative reasoning. Rajan connects all three — his strongest representation is extended to the policy layer. But the IRF chain is publicly driven and moves quickly — Siti's sentence completion test is the only individual accountability built into the sequence, and 90 seconds may not be enough for her to reconstruct the conceptual link if it has not yet formed.

choices.label: Self-check representation consistency rubric: circle the link you cannot make
choices.elaboration: Write a three-step consistency checklist on the board: '(1) PED value — calculated correctly with sign? (Quantity change on top, price change on bottom. Negative if quantity fell when price rose.) (2) Demand curve gradient — consistent with PED value? (Steep if |PED| < 1, flat if |PED| > 1.) (3) Policy sentence — uses PED value as justification, not just 'inelastic' or 'elastic'? (e.g., 'PED = −0.4, which is less than 1, so demand is inelastic — consumers bear most of the tax burden.')' Ask all students: 'Check your three representations against each other using this rubric. Do not circle the representation — circle the *link* between two representations that you cannot make yet. The gap is in the connection, not the individual piece.' Give 3 minutes. Then circulate specifically to Siti: 'Your curve gradient is now right. Show me the calculation that gives you that curve — what PED value does a steep curve represent?'
choices.tradeoff: The consistency rubric is metacognitive — it asks students to check not whether each representation is correct in isolation, but whether the three are coherent with each other. This targets the specific challenge of the lesson precisely: Nur may have a correct formula and a correct curve but a policy sentence that does not reference the PED value. Siti's rubric check will show she can draw the correct curve (step 2) but her PED value from the formula (step 1) is wrong because of the reversal — pinpointing exactly where the chain breaks. The follow-up question to Siti — 'show me the calculation that gives you that curve' — forces her to derive the formula from her own correct graph rather than memorise which way round it goes. But the rubric does not teach the missing link — it identifies it. Students who identify a gap they cannot make still need a next step to address it.

C.consequence: The IRF chain runs and the room follows. Rajan responds to the opening question: 'PED = −0.4, steep curve, tax — consumers bear the burden.' Nur says: 'I agree — my graph shows inelastic.' Wei Jie raises a genuine question: 'Does it matter how steep? Like, is there a threshold?' Siti echoes Nur: 'I agree, the graph shows inelastic.' From the verbal evidence across the class: 7 students connect all three representations in their responses — they reference the number, describe the curve, and give a policy implication. 11 students express two of the three — most commonly the curve and the policy, without the specific PED number. 6 students express only one representation — typically the verbal policy claim without a formula or a graph. Siti can describe the demand curve correctly when asked, but when you ask her to connect it to her formula, she cannot — the formula is still reversed and giving her a different value than her curve implies. The IRF chain has produced partial bridges across the class, but the depth varies. Siti's formula reversal is still blocking her chain, and the whole-class format has not given her the individual attention to correct the conceptual error. 18 minutes remain.

choices.label: AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) re-paired by representation evidence from the IRF (Initiate-Response-Feedback) chain
choices.elaboration: Restructure pairs using what you observed in the IRF chain — not pre-assigned learning styles, but the specific representation profile you saw in each student's response. Pair students who linked all three representations (like Rajan) with students who expressed only one (like Siti). Pair students with two representations (like Nur and Wei Jie) with each other. Give the AQS structure specifically: 'Affirm the representation your partner is strongest in. Question — can they connect it to the one they have not used? Suggest — the specific link.' For the Rajan-Siti pairing, brief Rajan specifically: 'Your job is not to tell Siti the formula — it is to ask her. Ask her: looking at your diagram, what is the percentage change in quantity demanded and where does it go in the formula? Let her derive it from her own graph.' For the Nur-Wei Jie pairing: 'Nur, show Wei Jie how the steepness of the curve tells you whether PED is greater or less than 1. Wei Jie, use your policy reasoning to explain why that threshold matters for tax policy.'
choices.tradeoff: Pairing by the representation profile observed in the IRF chain — not by pre-assigned roles or assumed learning styles — means the pairs are formed on evidence gathered in this lesson, not on assumptions. The Rajan-Siti pairing is designed to address Siti's conceptual formula error specifically: Rajan asking rather than telling forces Siti to derive the formula from her own correct graph, which is more likely to correct the conceptual misunderstanding than direct instruction. The Nur-Wei Jie pairing extends both students toward their weaker representation through each other's strength. But the success of the Rajan-Siti pairing depends on Rajan following the 'ask, don't tell' brief — if he provides the formula directly, Siti's conceptual error may remain uncorrected even after she writes the right answer.

choices.label: Solo three-representation synthesis: new data, all three independently
choices.elaboration: Stop the whole-class chain and ask all students to work individually with new data that prevents copying from the board example. Write on the board: 'New data: a 5% rise in price leads to a 12% fall in quantity demanded. Independently: (1) Calculate PED and show every step, including the sign. (2) Draw the demand curve and label its gradient — is it steep or flat? (3) Write one sentence: what this PED value means for a government considering an excise tax on this good.' Give 3 minutes. Collect 5–6 written responses as the exit evidence — do not grade, use as diagnostic. Tell students: 'New numbers — no copying from the worked example on the board. The goal is to connect all three, not to get a perfect answer in each.'
choices.tradeoff: New numbers prevent copying from the board example, which means the exit synthesis is an honest individual measure of representational integration under independent conditions. Siti's response will show whether any of the IRF chain — or the earlier triad or pair work — has corrected her formula reversal, or whether the conceptual error persists when she works without peer support. A PED of −2.4 (elastic) also requires a flat demand curve and a different policy implication from the earlier inelastic example — so the synthesis tests whether students can adjust all three representations for a different case, not just reproduce the same values. But 3 minutes at the end of a lesson is limited time to produce all three representations independently, and students who were building understanding through the IRF chain may need more time than students who are reproducing familiar steps.

A1.outcome: You write the three-representation chain on the board, erase it after 90 seconds, and ask students to reconnect their own work. Nur looks at the space where the negative sign appeared on the board and immediately corrects her answer — she rewrites −0.4 and adds a note: 'The negative sign matters — quantity fell when price rose.' She then connects her formula to her graph by annotating the curve: 'Steep gradient = small %ΔQd for given %ΔP = PED less than 1.' Her three representations are now consistent and she has made the link between them explicit. Wei Jie uses the board model to check his arithmetic — he identifies his slip and corrects it to −0.4. He then writes a policy sentence that references the PED value for the first time: 'PED = −0.4, which is less than 1, so demand is inelastic — consumers bear most of the tax burden.' His three representations are now consistent. Rajan draws his demand curve more precisely after the board model gives him a reference gradient. He then asks you — without prompting — whether he can draw the tax revenue area on the demand diagram. You say yes. He draws a tax-inclusive price rectangle and calculates the total tax revenue, extending beyond the task without any new resource needed. Siti watches the board model carefully and rewrites her formula after the erase — but she writes %ΔP ÷ %ΔQd again, the same reversal. When you circulate and ask her 'Which number goes on top?', she points to 10% (the price change) and places it on top. The board model showed her the correct formula, but without understanding why quantity is the numerator, the erase made the correction disappear. Her curve gradient is now correct after the board reference, but the formula and the curve are still inconsistent — she has a correct-looking steep curve but a formula giving PED = 2.5 (the reciprocal of the correct value). On a class-level check: 19 of 24 students have consistent representations across all three, up from 9. The board model has efficiently corrected sign errors and arithmetic slips for the middle group. But Siti's formula reversal — a conceptual error, not a mechanical one — was not addressed by seeing the correct formula displayed and then erased. She needs to understand what the formula measures, not just which way round it goes. 14 minutes remain.
A1.tradeoff: The board model with immediate erase is efficient and addresses the most common mechanical errors — the missing negative sign and arithmetic slips in the percentage change calculation. Nur and Wei Jie made clear gains and can now connect all three representations consistently. Rajan extended himself in-flight without any new resource. But the erase exposes the limit of display-based feedback for conceptual errors: Siti can copy the correct formula from the board and then revert to the reversal immediately after the erase, because she has not understood why quantity is in the numerator. The lesson has served 19 of 24 students well at the mechanical and consistency level, but leaves Siti's conceptual gap unaddressed.
A1.reflection:
Why did you choose to erase the board model immediately rather than leaving it up as a reference? What were you hoping students would do when the model disappeared?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? 19 of 24 students now have consistent representations, but Siti's formula reversal persists after seeing the correct formula twice. What does this tell you about the difference between a mechanical error and a conceptual error in how you design feedback?

How do you design the next lesson so that Siti — and students like her — understand why quantity is the numerator in the PED formula, rather than which way round the formula goes?

A2.outcome: Pairs swap their written work and apply the AQS cross-representation consistency check. Rajan is paired with a student who has a correct formula and a correct verbal explanation but a flat demand curve for an inelastic good — the inconsistency between the number and the diagram. Rajan's AQS is immediate and precise: 'Your calculation is right — PED = −0.4 and your policy sentence is correct. But your demand curve is flat. For PED = −0.4, the curve should be steep — a flat curve shows elastic demand, where a small price rise causes a big fall in quantity. Check the gradient.' His partner looks at the two representations together for the first time and sees the inconsistency. He corrects the curve. Nur's partner notices the missing negative sign in Nur's formula and questions it: 'Your gradient is right — steep for inelastic. But your PED answer is 0.4 positive. Does the sign matter?' Nur pauses and then says: 'The negative tells you quantity fell when price rose — it should be −0.4.' She corrects it. Wei Jie's partner notices he has no diagram at all: 'You have the formula and the policy sentence — but your diagram is blank. The AQS says: your verbal reasoning is the strongest, but what does this look like on a curve? Suggest: try drawing a steep curve — PED = −0.4 is inelastic, which means steep.' Wei Jie draws a steep demand curve with axes for the first time. Siti's partner reads her formula — %ΔP ÷ %ΔQd — and pauses. He has the correct formula himself. He says: 'I think your formula is the wrong way around? I have %ΔQd on top.' Siti says: 'Why is the quantity change on top?' Her partner says: 'Because... that is what the formula is.' He cannot explain the conceptual reason. Siti writes the correct formula in her notes because her partner has it right — but when you ask her a moment later to redo the calculation on the back of her page without looking at her partner's work, she writes the reversal again. On a class-level check: 17 of 24 students have resolved at least one cross-representation inconsistency through the peer critique. 12 of 24 now have consistent representations across all three. 14 minutes remain.
A2.tradeoff: AQS cross-representation peer critique is effective at catching inconsistencies that single-representation checking misses — students who had individually correct representations but inconsistent connections were caught specifically by the Question step. Rajan extended a peer who had correct numbers but a wrong diagram. Nur corrected her sign error through a targeted peer question. Wei Jie drew a diagram for the first time under peer prompting. But Siti's partner could identify that her formula was wrong but could not explain why — he gave her the correct answer without the conceptual understanding behind it, and the error returned immediately under independent conditions. Peer feedback corrects mechanical errors well but requires the peer to understand the conceptual reason for a correction, not just the correct answer.
A2.reflection:
Why did you frame the AQS around cross-representation consistency rather than asking pairs to check each representation individually? What difference did that make?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? 12 of 24 students now have consistent representations, but Siti's error returned immediately after peer correction. What does this tell you about what makes peer feedback durable versus transient?

How do you design the next step so that Siti understands why the formula is structured the way it is — not just which way round it goes?

B1.outcome: You nominate Nur: 'Nur, you drew the correct curve before you calculated. How does the steepness of the curve tell you something about the size of the PED number?' Nur looks at her graph and responds slowly: 'A steep curve means... for a big move in price, the quantity barely changes. So the quantity change is small and the price change is big — the ratio is small.' You build on this: 'So the numerator is small and the denominator is large — what does that tell us about the PED number?' Nur connects: 'The PED number is less than 1 — inelastic.' You extend to Wei Jie: 'Wei Jie, if I told you PED = −2.5 — describe the curve without drawing it.' Wei Jie thinks: 'The percentage change in quantity is bigger than the percentage change in price — so the curve is flat. Elastic.' He is correct. You extend to Rajan: 'Rajan — connect both. If the curve is flat, what does that tell a government about taxing this good?' Rajan gives a complete answer: 'If demand is elastic, a tax would significantly reduce quantity demanded — the deadweight loss is larger and tax revenue may fall if the fall in quantity outweighs the tax per unit. A flat curve means taxing this good is costly in terms of market distortion.' You then turn to Siti: 'Complete this sentence in writing, independently: A steeper demand curve means PED is ___ because ___. Take 90 seconds.' Siti writes: 'A steeper demand curve means PED is less than 1 because the quantity change is smaller than the price change.' Her sentence is correct and — crucially — she has placed quantity change before price change in her reasoning, which mirrors the correct numerator position. You ask her immediately: 'Now write your formula — which number goes on top?' She writes %ΔQd ÷ %ΔP for the first time, correctly. The IRF chain that used the graph as the entry point into the formula has corrected her conceptual reversal by giving her the reason — quantity is the numerator because it is the response being measured. On a class-level check: 18 of 24 students can now connect all three representations with correct internal consistency. 14 minutes remain.
B1.tradeoff: Using Nur's graphical strength as the entry point for the formula — reversing the typical instruction order — produced the conceptual correction in Siti that the board model and peer AQS had failed to achieve. By building the formula from the graph ('the quantity change is small and the price change is big, so the ratio is small — PED < 1'), the IRF chain gave Siti the reason why quantity is the numerator, not just the correct position. Rajan's extension to the deadweight loss and market distortion argument stretched him into policy application without any new task. But the IRF chain is driven by confident verbal contributors — it works best when students who are strong in different representations are present and willing to contribute publicly. In classes where representation strengths are less evenly distributed, the chain may stall.
B1.reflection:
Why did you start the IRF chain with Nur's graphical explanation rather than with the formula or with the policy implication? What did reversing the typical order achieve?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Siti's formula reversal appears to be corrected — she wrote the formula correctly for the first time after the IRF chain. What does this tell you about the difference between correcting an error by showing the correct answer and correcting it by building the reasoning from a representation the student already understands?

How do you design IRF chains in a diverse-profiles class so that each student's strength becomes a building block for the class's understanding — not just a chance for the strongest student to demonstrate?

B2.outcome: Students apply the consistency rubric to their own work. Rajan completes it quickly: all three steps are internally consistent, and he circles no gap. He then annotates the rubric: 'Extension — step 3 could include: how does the PED value change the size of the tax incidence on each party?' He draws a tax incidence diagram in the margin. Nur works through the rubric carefully. Step 1: her PED value is 0.4 — positive, missing the sign. She circles the link between step 1 and step 2: 'My number is missing the negative sign. My curve is correct.' She corrects the sign to −0.4. Step 2: her curve gradient is consistent with PED < 1. Step 3: her policy sentence says 'inelastic goods are good to tax' — she circles the link between steps 1 and 3: 'My sentence doesn't use the number to justify.' She rewrites: 'PED = −0.4, which is less than 1, so demand is inelastic — consumers bear most of the tax burden.' Wei Jie has no diagram — he circles the link between step 1 and step 2: 'I have the number and the policy sentence but no curve.' He draws a steep demand curve for the first time. Siti's rubric reveals the chain break precisely: her curve gradient is now correct (step 2), but her PED formula gives 2.5 rather than 0.4 because the numerator and denominator are reversed (step 1). She circles the step 1–step 2 link. When you follow up: 'Your curve is right — show me the calculation that gives you that curve. If the curve is steep, is the quantity change bigger or smaller than the price change?', Siti says 'smaller.' You ask: 'So which one goes on top of the fraction — the bigger or the smaller?' Siti writes: '%ΔQd ÷ %ΔP = 4% ÷ 10% = −0.4.' Her formula is correct for the first time. The follow-up question — deriving the formula from her own correct curve — has corrected the conceptual reversal. On a class-level check: 17 of 24 students have identified and addressed at least one cross-representation inconsistency. 14 minutes remain.
B2.tradeoff: The consistency rubric is metacognitive — it asks students to locate the gap between representations rather than within each representation. This shifts the thinking from 'is each part right?' to 'are the parts connected?' Nur identified her sign error by comparing steps 1 and 2. Wei Jie drew his first diagram by identifying the step 1–step 2 gap. Siti's rubric pinpointed the exact break in her chain — the inconsistency between her correct curve and her reversed formula — and the follow-up question derived the correction from her own correct curve rather than telling her the right formula. But the rubric identifies gaps; it does not teach the missing links. Students who circle a gap still need a next step. The rubric works best as the diagnostic for a targeted follow-up — not as a standalone correction.
B2.reflection:
Why did you design the rubric around cross-representation links rather than asking students to correct each representation individually? What did 'circle the link you cannot make' achieve that 'correct your formula' would not?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Siti's formula reversal was finally corrected through a question that derived the formula from her own correct curve. What does this tell you about what Siti needed — and why the board model and the peer AQS had not given it to her?

How do you design the next PED lesson so that all students encounter the three-way connection from the start — rather than defaulting to their preferred representation and discovering the gaps at the 25-minute mark?

C1.outcome: You form the pairs by representation profile from the IRF evidence. Rajan is paired with Siti. You brief Rajan: 'Your job is not to tell Siti the formula — ask her. Start with her diagram.' Rajan looks at Siti's steep demand curve and asks: 'Your curve is steep — is that elastic or inelastic?' Siti says: 'Inelastic.' Rajan: 'So is the quantity change bigger or smaller than the price change?' Siti: 'Smaller.' Rajan: 'So in the formula — which one goes on top? The bigger or the smaller?' Siti pauses, then writes %ΔQd ÷ %ΔP. She calculates: 4% ÷ 10% = 0.4. Rajan: 'Almost — what sign?' Siti: 'Negative, because quantity fell.' She writes −0.4. She looks at the formula and her curve and says: 'Oh — the formula is the curve. The steep gradient means the number is small.' Rajan has guided Siti to derive the formula from her own correct graph rather than memorising which way round it goes, and she has produced a genuine conceptual correction. Nur and Wei Jie are paired together. Nur shows Wei Jie the steepness link: 'Look at my curve — the gradient tells you the ratio. Steep means the quantity change is small relative to the price change — that is what PED < 1 looks like.' Wei Jie connects it to his verbal reasoning: 'So if PED = −2.5, the curve is flat because the percentage quantity change is more than twice the price change — consumers are very responsive.' He draws the flat curve correctly. Nur uses Wei Jie's policy fluency to strengthen her own policy sentence — she incorporates the PED value as a justification for the first time. On a class-level check: 20 of 24 students have consistent three-way representations by the end of the pairing. 14 minutes remain.
C1.tradeoff: Pairing by observed representation profile — not pre-assigned style categories — placed the right students together for the right reason. Rajan's 'ask, don't tell' approach guided Siti to derive the formula from her correct graph, producing a conceptual correction rather than a mechanical copy. The Nur-Wei Jie pairing extended both students toward their weaker representation through each other's genuine strength — Nur's graphical explanation gave Wei Jie the visual he had never attempted, and Wei Jie's policy fluency gave Nur a model for evidence-based policy reasoning. But the success of the Rajan-Siti pairing depended entirely on Rajan following the 'ask, don't tell' brief. If he had given Siti the formula directly, the correction would have been mechanical rather than conceptual — and the error would likely have returned under independent conditions.
C1.reflection:
Why did you brief Rajan specifically to ask rather than tell? What would have happened if Rajan had simply given Siti the correct formula?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? 20 of 24 students have consistent representations, and Siti appears to have made a genuine conceptual correction — she connected the formula to the curve independently. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for a peer to correct a conceptual error, as opposed to a mechanical one?

How do you use IRF evidence to form pairs that address specific representation gaps — not just general readiness levels? What information from the IRF chain made the Rajan-Siti and Nur-Wei Jie pairings possible?

C2.outcome: The room goes quiet as students work individually on the exit synthesis. New data: a 5% rise in price leads to a 12% fall in quantity demanded — PED = −12% ÷ 5% = −2.4, which is elastic, requiring a flat demand curve and a different policy implication from the earlier inelastic example. Rajan completes all three elements in under 2 minutes: 'PED = −2.4, elastic. Flat demand curve — label: large quantity response to small price rise. Policy: government should not tax this good — a tax would significantly reduce quantity demanded, creating a large deadweight loss and potentially reducing total tax revenue if the revenue effect is outweighed by the quantity effect.' He extends unprompted to elasticity and tax revenue trade-offs. Nur produces a flat demand curve correctly — she has adjusted the gradient for the elastic case — and writes PED = −2.4 with the correct sign. Her policy sentence says: 'PED = −2.4, elastic, so consumers are very responsive to price — a tax would significantly reduce quantity demanded.' She has used the PED value as an explicit justification for the first time. Wei Jie writes his policy sentence first, then attempts the formula: '%ΔQd ÷ %ΔP = −12% ÷ 5% = −2.4.' He writes the formula correctly — the triad explanation of the formula has held. He draws a flat curve last, after the formula, correctly. Siti writes PED = %ΔP ÷ %ΔQd = 5% ÷ 12% = 0.42 — the reversal has persisted under independent conditions. Her curve is flat — she has correctly identified that PED > 1 should give a flat curve. Her policy sentence is blank. She has a correct curve and an inconsistent formula: the graph tells her demand is elastic, but her formula is giving her a value less than 1 if applied correctly to the reversed fraction. The exit synthesis gives you a clear diagnostic: the IRF chain produced partial bridges but did not correct Siti's formula reversal under individual, unsupported conditions. The next lesson needs to open with a targeted individual intervention for Siti before moving to any new application. 12 minutes remain.
C2.tradeoff: The exit synthesis with new data is the most honest diagnostic available — new numbers prevent copying, and the elastic case (PED = −2.4) requires a different curve gradient and different policy implication, testing whether students can adjust all three representations for a genuinely new scenario rather than reproducing familiar values. Rajan, Nur, and Wei Jie demonstrated genuine representational integration under independent conditions. But Siti's formula reversal returned immediately when peer support was removed — confirming that the IRF chain gave her access to the correct reasoning through verbal exchange, but did not build the independent understanding needed to apply it alone. The exit synthesis is highly informative as a next-lesson diagnostic, but it has confirmed the gap rather than closed it.
C2.reflection:
Why did you use new data — a 5% price rise and 12% quantity fall — rather than asking students to redo the original calculation for the exit synthesis? What did the elastic case add that the original inelastic example could not test?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan, Nur, and Wei Jie showed genuine representational integration independently. But Siti's formula reversal returned under independent conditions. What does this tell you about the difference between understanding built through discussion and understanding that holds under independent conditions?

How will you open the next PED lesson for Siti specifically — and what sequence of questions, not explanations, will help her build the formula from her own correct graphical understanding so that the correction is durable?
